one_line_takeaway: A domain-tooled REACT agent (SLYP) with binary-exploration, COM-adapter, and live-debugger MCP tools finds all 64 benchmarked COM race vulnerabilities with 2-4 false positives and debugger-verifies PoCs for up to 67.5% of cases -- while default Claude Code/Codex miss up to 15 vulnerabilities and verify zero PoCs, and a reproduced static analyzer (COMRACE++) never exceeds 35/64 -- and the pipeline has already produced 39 real zero-days (23 CVEs, $203K in MSRC bounties).
key_finding: On the 20-object COM benchmark (Table II), SLYP recovers all 64/64 vulnerable entry functions with only 2 (GPT-5.4) or 4 (Opus 4.6) false positives, versus default CODEX 61/64 (5 FP) and default Claude Code 49/64 (17 FP, i.e. 2.5x and 4.3x SLYP's FP count respectively). For validation (40 cases), SLYP verifies debugger-confirmed PoCs for 23/40 (GPT-5.4) and 27/40 (Opus 4.6, 67.5%), while default production agents verify 0/40 in both cases -- a result the authors describe as structural (holds for both model backbones) rather than model-specific, tied to lack of COM activation metadata and execution feedback. Giving production agents the full SLYP toolset (Table IV/V ablation) closes most of the discovery gap (CC+Tools reaches 62/64, CODEX+Tools reaches 64/64) and substantially raises validation (CC+Tools 26/40, CODEX+Tools 22/40), while removing tools from SLYP itself degrades it (SLYP -Tools: 62/64 and 59/64 discovery; 1-2/40 validation), isolating tool support rather than scaffold identity as the main driver of the gap. Against the reproduced static analyzer (Table VII), no COMRACE++ configuration recovers more than 35/64 functions or fully solves more than 11/20 objects (each configuration leaves at least 5 objects with zero recovered functions), and the false-positive/coverage tradeoff never breaks 147-216 false positives across sweeps -- SLYP's weakest tested model (Haiku 4.5, 42/64) still outperforms every COMRACE++ configuration. Model sweep (Table VI) shows discovery is comparatively robust across models (Haiku 4.5 still finds 42/64 with 7 FP) but validation degrades sharply with model strength (Haiku 4.5: 12.5% verified vs Opus 4.6: 67.5%). Beyond the benchmark (RQ4), SLYP found 39 zero-days total (31 COM/RPC across 12 services, 8 kernel across 4 driver components spanning win32k.sys, ntoskrnl.exe, afd.sys, Http.sys and 3 bug classes), all disclosed to MSRC, with 23 assigned CVEs and $203,000 in bounty awards to date.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SLYP, a from-scratch REACT-style LLM agent pipeline for end-to-end vulnerability discovery and validation on commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) Windows binaries, specifically Windows COM services and (as a generalization test) Windows kernel drivers. SLYP has two stages: Stage 1 (Discovery) uses MCP-exposed binary exploration tools (decompilation with automatic virtual-dispatch/vtable resolution, disassembly, cross-references, call-graph navigation) plus an optional target adapter (COM registry/interface/security-descriptor inspection, skeleton PoC generation) to hypothesize race-induced memory-corruption vulnerabilities (use-after-free/double-free) in COM entry functions; Stage 2 (Validation) uses dynamic debugging MCP tools (PoC compile/execute/crash-capture against a live QEMU-hosted Windows 11 VM with a WinDbg-driven debugger) to iteratively write, compile, and run C++ proof-of-concept exploits until a debugger-verified crash is captured. The agent framework also contributes engineering components for long-horizon agentic security work: checkpointed auto-compaction, a BM25-retrieved file-based memory system, and stop-time/mid-trajectory task-verification middleware. The paper presents this as both a system contribution (19.9K LOC agent framework + 8.7K LOC across three MCP servers) and an empirical study answering whether frontier LLM agents can reason about vulnerabilities directly from stripped/decompiled COTS binaries (as opposed to source code, which prior agentic-security evaluations assumed access to).
threat_model: Local privilege escalation (LPE) on Windows via COM race-condition memory-corruption bugs (UAF, double-free) and, in the generalization experiment, kernel-driver bugs (UAF, out-of-bounds, buffer overflow). The attacker is a low-privileged (or sandboxed/medium-integrity) local client that can activate a COM object via CoCreateInstance/RoActivateInstance and issue concurrent RPC method invocations on a service that registers as a multi-threaded apartment (MTA) with no framework-provided locking; a data race on a shared this+offset field (read/free, write/free, free/free) yields UAF or double-free, giving the attacker code execution at the privilege level of the hosting service (paper cites security-boundary escalations such as LOW to MEDIUM, LOW to SYSTEM, and SANDBOX to SYSTEM in Tables III and VIII). The paper's own artifact (SLYP) is offense-oriented, positioned as vulnerability research/AI-security-capability assessment rather than a defense; the agent itself is not treated as an attack surface (no discussion of prompt injection into decompiled code/binary metadata as a threat to the agent).
limitations: Authors' own stated threats to validity: (1) Internal validity/data leakage -- all zero-day patches post-date the models' training cutoff (Aug 2025) so those cases are leakage-free, but the 8 included 1-day benchmark objects may predate the cutoff, though the authors argue leakage is impractical since Microsoft ships cumulative binary patches without field offsets/object layouts/sync patterns and SLYP works from decompiled binaries, not source or advisories. (2) External validity -- the controlled study covers only one platform (Windows COTS binaries) and one bug family (race-induced UAF/double-free in COM), though the RQ4 kernel extension is offered as partial evidence of generalizability. Additional limitations evident from the text itself: static/dynamic vtable resolution is explicitly incomplete (vtables loaded dynamically from external binaries and polymorphic multi-class initialization remain unresolved); discovery scoring uses each configuration's best of 3 runs pooled per object (a best-of-N metric that can overstate single-run reliability, acknowledged as necessary because discovery lacks validation feedback and is nondeterministic); validation is scored on a single attempt per case rather than best-of-N; and the study is confined to memory-corruption race conditions, not other COTS vulnerability classes (e.g., logic bugs, type confusion beyond what races produce, or non-race UAF).

datasets_used: Custom 20-object Windows COM benchmark across 10 services, 379 entry functions, 64 manually confirmed vulnerable (built by the authors for this study, ground truth pooled across SLYP/CODEX/CC candidates plus independent 1-day reports and MSRC confirmation)
40 grouped vulnerability-validation cases derived from the 64 vulnerable functions
Original COMRACE dataset (used only to validate the COMRACE++ reproduction's detection parity with the published tool, not as a benchmark for SLYP itself)
quantitative_results: Discovery, GPT-5.4: SLYP 64/64 (2 FP) vs default CODEX 61/64 (5 FP)
Discovery, Opus 4.6: SLYP 64/64 (4 FP) vs default Claude Code 49/64 (17 FP)
Validation, GPT-5.4: SLYP 23/40 vs default CODEX 0/40
Validation, Opus 4.6: SLYP 27/40 (67.5%) vs default Claude Code 0/40
Tool ablation discovery: CODEX+Tools 64/64, CC+Tools 62/64 (vs SLYP 64/64 same models)
Tool ablation validation: CODEX+Sub 13/40, CODEX+Tools 22/40; CC+Sub 13/40, CC+Tools 26/40
Model sweep discovery: GPT-5.4 64/64, GPT-5.3-codex 64/64, GPT-5.4-mini 59/64, Opus 4.6 64/64, Sonnet 4.6 64/64, Haiku 4.5 42/64 (7 FP)
Model sweep validation (verified rate): GPT-5.4 57.5%, GPT-5.3-codex 57.5%, GPT-5.4-mini 37.5%, Opus 4.6 67.5%, Sonnet 4.6 40.0%, Haiku 4.5 12.5%

Static-analyzer comparison: COMRACE++ configurations recover between 30 and 35 of 64 functions with 147-216 false positives; best coverage config (+E1/E2) 35/64 with 216 FP; best precision config (+E2/E3/E4) 30/64 with 147 FP; no configuration fully solves more than 11/20 objects vs SLYP's 20/20
Real-world yield to date: 39 zero-days (31 COM/RPC across 12 services, 8 kernel across 4 driver components), 23 assigned CVEs, $203,000 in bounty awards
RQ4 additional findings beyond the 20-object benchmark: 7 more COM/RPC zero-days (6 UAF, 1 DF) and 8 kernel zero-days (3 UAF, 3 OOB, 2 OVF) spanning win32k.sys, ntoskrnl.exe, afd.sys, Http.sys
baselines_compared: OpenAI Codex CLI (GPT-5.4) -- default minimal binary-navigation config and +Tools variant
Anthropic Claude Code (Opus 4.6) -- default minimal binary-navigation config and +Tools variant

COMRACE++ -- authors' from-scratch reproduction of COMRACE (USENIX Security'22 static COM race detector) with correctness fixes M1-M3 and optional enhancements E1 (CFG traversal), E2 (per-field lock-set tracking), E3 (R/R pair filtering), E4 (dereferenced pointer recursion)
SLYP itself across four additional model backbones (GPT-5.3-codex, GPT-5.4-mini, Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 4.5) for model-generalization ablation
